AI.conclusion: Supports deploying lightweight architectures for malaria diagnosis in resource-constrained settings but highlights vulnerability of post-hoc explanations as a critical consideration for responsible clinical deployment.

title: DARTS: Distribution-Aware Active Rollout Trajectory Shaping for Accelerating LLM Reinforcement Learning
comment: 16 pages, 14 figures, 5 tables. Accepted to ICML 2026
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become pivotal for improving model capabilities yet suffers from rollout efficiency bottlenecks due to the long-tail response length distribution. While existing works mitigate the impact of long tails via prompt-level tail scheduling, we focus on the root source of inefficiency: the distribution itself. Specifically, we characterize the long-tail distribution at a finer granularity, identifying intra-prompt long tails, and revealing that they frequently consist of ineffective verbosity. To address this, we propose a novel paradigm of active distribution shaping to shape the rollout distribution towards conciseness and certainty, thereby fundamentally resolving tail-induced overheads. We achieve this through a distribution-aware trajectory sampling mechanism, which selects trajectories from a redundant exploration space for each prompt, and an adaptive redundancy allocation scheme to maximize both shaping effectiveness and system efficiency. Experiments demonstrate significant acceleration over state-of-the-art systems by up to 1.77x without compromising model performance.

AI.tldr: RL acceleration through active shaping of long-tail response distributions by reducing intra-prompt verbosity and uncertainty, achieving 1.77x speedup without performance loss.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement Learning suffers from rollout efficiency bottlenecks due to long-tail response length distributions. Existing approaches only address this at the prompt level, but the fundamental inefficiency lies in the distribution itself, particularly intra-prompt long tails that contain ineffective verbosity.
AI.method: Proposes active distribution shaping to make rollouts more concise and certain. Uses a distribution-aware trajectory sampling mechanism that selects trajectories from redundant exploration space for each prompt, and an adaptive redundancy allocation scheme to balance shaping effectiveness with system efficiency.
AI.result: Achieves significant acceleration over state-of-the-art systems by up to 1.77x without compromising model performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of addressing intra-prompt long tails.
AI.conclusion: Active distribution shaping addresses the root cause of RL rollout inefficiency by reducing verbosity and uncertainty in long-tail distributions, fundamentally resolving overheads while maintaining performance.

title: FLAG: Flow Policy MaxEnt-RL by Latent Augmented Guidance
summary:
Maximum entropy reinforcement learning (MaxEnt-RL) enables robust exploration, yet practical implementations often restrict policies to simple Gaussians.
  While recent approaches incorporate expressive generative policies via importance-weighted supervised learning, they are prone to importance weight collapse, which limits their scalability in high-dimensional action spaces.
  Our key insight is to mitigate this limitation by localizing the sampling region, avoiding the weight degeneracy induced by importance sampling over the entire action space.
  To instantiate this insight, we introduce \textbf{FLAG} (\textbf{F}low policy with \textbf{L}atent-\textbf{A}ugmented \textbf{G}uidance).
  FLAG augments the state space with a flow latent variable and optimizes a provably consistent proxy MaxEnt-RL objective.
  We empirically demonstrate that FLAG enables expressive policy optimization with limited importance samples and scales to high-dimensional control tasks.
  Furthermore, FLAG achieves state-of-the-art performance across challenging benchmarks. Our project webpage: https://flag-rl.github.io/

AI.tldr: FLAG introduces a flow policy with latent-augmented guidance to mitigate importance weight collapse in MaxEnt-RL, enabling expressive policy optimization that scales to high-dimensional control tasks.
AI.motivation: Maximum entropy reinforcement learning (MaxEnt-RL) enables robust exploration but practical implementations restrict policies to simple Gaussians. Recent approaches using expressive generative policies via importance-weighted supervised learning suffer from importance weight collapse, limiting scalability in high-dimensional action spaces.
AI.method: FLAG (Flow policy with Latent-Augmented Guidance) augments the state space with a flow latent variable and optimizes a provably consistent proxy MaxEnt-RL objective. The key insight is to localize the sampling region to avoid weight degeneracy induced by importance sampling over the entire action space.
AI.result: FLAG enables expressive policy optimization with limited importance samples and scales to high-dimensional control tasks. It achieves state-of-the-art performance across challenging benchmarks, demonstrating practical scalability.
AI.conclusion: FLAG successfully addresses the importance weight collapse problem in MaxEnt-RL with expressive policies, providing a scalable solution for high-dimensional control tasks through localized sampling and latent space augmentation.

title: Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior for Personalized User Preferences
summary: Federated Learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving pathway for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs); however, existing frameworks typically enforce a monolithic reward model, inevitably averaging out inherently conflicting user preferences (e.g., helpfulness vs. harmlessness). While Variational Preference Learning (VPL) offers a pathway to personalization, adapting it to decentralized settings presents a fundamental challenge: posterior collapse driven by severe local data scarcity and heterogeneity. In this paper, we propose Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior (FedVPA-GP), a framework designed to disentangle diverse preferences without compromising privacy. To stabilize variational inference, we introduce a Federated Mixture Prior that enables clients to leverage the aggregate population distribution as a dynamic prior. Furthermore, we incorporate an Orthogonal Loss that explicitly enforces the separation of preference prototypes in the latent space. Experiments on the HH-RLHF dataset demonstrate that FedVPA-GP significantly outperforms monolithic baselines, successfully disentangling conflicting user intents and enabling dynamic preference switching.

AI.tldr: FedVPA-GP: A federated learning framework that uses variational preference learning with Gumbel-Softmax prior to personalize LLM alignment while handling conflicting user preferences and preventing posterior collapse in decentralized settings.
AI.motivation: Existing federated learning frameworks for LLM alignment enforce monolithic reward models that average out conflicting user preferences (e.g., helpfulness vs. harmlessness). Variational Preference Learning offers personalization but faces posterior collapse when adapted to decentralized settings due to severe local data scarcity and heterogeneity.
AI.method: Propose Federated Variational Preference Alignment with Gumbel-Softmax Prior (FedVPA-GP) with two key components: 1) Federated Mixture Prior that enables clients to leverage aggregate population distribution as dynamic prior to stabilize variational inference, and 2) Orthogonal Loss that explicitly enforces separation of preference prototypes in latent space.
AI.result: Experiments on HH-RLHF dataset show FedVPA-GP significantly outperforms monolithic baselines, successfully disentangles conflicting user intents, and enables dynamic preference switching.
AI.conclusion: FedVPA-GP provides an effective framework for privacy-preserving personalized LLM alignment that handles diverse and conflicting user preferences in federated settings while preventing posterior collapse through innovative prior design and orthogonal regularization.

title: Chain-of-Thought and Compressed Looped Transformers: A Memory-Budget Separation
summary:
Chain-of-thought prompting and looped Transformers both give a fixed model more test-time computation, but they differ in what they remember. Chain-of-thought stores intermediate state in generated tokens that remain in the context, whereas a looped Transformer carries state through recurrent hidden activations. We argue that this persistent mutable memory is a central resource for test-time reasoning.
  We compare three memory regimes, the compressed latent loop, the full sequence-state loop, and the chain-of-thought scratchpad. Our main result shows that a compressed loop is limited by the size of its recurrent state. Running the loop longer adds computation but does not by itself create a growing scratchpad, so a loop with a small recurrent state remains a small-space reasoner even when run for many steps. Under a standard complexity assumption, such loops cannot decide problems that are P-complete under logspace reductions, whereas polynomial-length chain-of-thought can.
  The separation is specific to compressed loops, as full sequence-state loops carry state at every input position and live in a memory-rich regime closer to explicit scratchpads. Controlled pointer-chasing and associative-recall sweeps illustrate this memory-budget view, with performance sensitive to whether the persistent-state budget matches the task's working-memory demand.

AI.tldr: Persistent mutable memory is crucial for reasoning: compressed loops have fixed memory limits despite longer computation, while chain-of-thought's explicit token memory scales polynomially.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental differences between chain-of-thought reasoning (explicit intermediate tokens) and looped Transformers (recurrent hidden states) in terms of memory persistence and capacity, and how these differences impact reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis comparing three memory regimes: compressed latent loops (recurrent hidden states), full sequence-state loops (state at every position), and chain-of-thought scratchpads (explicit token memory). Examination using complexity theory and empirical tasks like pointer-chasing and associative recall.
AI.result: Compressed latent loops are limited by their fixed recurrent state size - running them longer adds computation but doesn't create growing memory. They cannot solve P-complete problems under logspace reductions, whereas polynomial-length chain-of-thought can. Full sequence-state loops provide more memory capacity similar to scratchpads.
AI.conclusion: The memory architecture of reasoning systems is crucial: compressed recurrent loops have fundamental limitations in solving problems requiring growing working memory, even with extended computation time, whereas chain-of-thought provides explicit token-based memory that scales polynomially with sequence length.

title: Inverse Reinforcement Learning without an Optimal Demonstrator: A Feasible Reward Set Approach
summary: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) typically assumes demonstrations from a single optimal demonstrator, but in many applications data come from multiple imperfect demonstrators with heterogeneous suboptimality levels. We study reward learning in this setting through a feasible-reward-set framework: for each demonstrator, we encode its declared suboptimality level as a linear constraint and intersect the resulting feasible sets across demonstrators. Our theoretical analysis shows that the joint feasible set shrinks monotonically as data are added, and we give an exact characterization of when a new demonstrator strictly tightens it. We further establish two recovery guarantees for the feasible reward set of the ground-truth optimal demonstrator: one bound depends on closeness to the optimal occupancy, while the other requires only sufficient coverage and no near-optimal demonstrator. On the practical side, we introduce strategies to address the inherent reward ambiguity in the obtained reward set and provide an offline algorithm with function approximation for high-dimensional environments. Experiments in tabular grid-world and large language model (LLM) fine-tuning settings are consistent with the theoretical predictions and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework over baselines.

AI.tldr: Multi-demonstrator IRL framework using feasible reward sets to handle heterogeneous suboptimal demonstrators, with theoretical guarantees and practical algorithms for reward learning from imperfect data.
AI.motivation: Traditional IRL assumes demonstrations from a single optimal agent, but real-world applications often involve data from multiple imperfect demonstrators with varying suboptimality levels. This mismatch motivates developing IRL methods that can handle heterogeneous, suboptimal demonstrations.
AI.method: Proposes a feasible-reward-set framework: encode each demonstrator's declared suboptimality level as linear constraints, intersect resulting feasible sets across demonstrators. Provides theoretical analysis of set shrinkage, introduces offline algorithm with function approximation for high-dimensional environments, and develops strategies to address reward ambiguity.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows joint feasible set shrinks monotonically with more data, with exact characterization of when new demonstrators tighten the set. Establishes two recovery guarantees: one based on closeness to optimal occupancy, another requiring only sufficient coverage without near-optimal demonstrator. Experimental validation in tabular grid-world and LLM fine-tuning settings shows effectiveness over baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed feasible-reward-set framework effectively handles multiple suboptimal demonstrators in IRL, provides theoretical guarantees for reward recovery, and demonstrates practical applicability in both tabular and high-dimensional settings including LLM fine-tuning.

title: Efficient and Uncertainty-Aware Diffusion Framework for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning (O2O-RL) leverages an offline, pre-trained policy to minimize costly online interactions. Although data-efficient, O2O-RL is susceptible to shifts between offline and online distributions. Existing work aims to mitigate the harm of this shift by finetuning the policy on trajectory data sampled from a diffusion model. Inspired by this line of work, we propose DUAL: an efficient \textbf{D}iffusion \textbf{U}ncertainty-\textbf{A}ware framework for offline-to-online reinforcement \textbf{L}earning. DUAL utilizes the prior knowledge of the diffusion model to distill a fast-sampling diffusion actor policy and transition model in the offline phase. DUAL also employs a Laplace approximation and distance transition-state-shift detection, thereby using uncertainty quantification to improve exploration versus exploitation in the online phase. We formally show that our actor loss with the Laplace approximation provides a proxy for a principled estimate of epistemic uncertainty. Empirically, DUAL improves the online expected return over O2O-RL baselines across multiple settings and environments.

AI.tldr: DUAL is a diffusion uncertainty-aware framework for offline-to-online RL that uses diffusion model prior knowledge, Laplace approximation for uncertainty quantification, and distance-based shift detection to improve online performance while minimizing costly interactions.
AI.motivation: Offline-to-Online RL is data-efficient but susceptible to distribution shifts between offline and online environments. Existing methods use diffusion models for trajectory data, but need better ways to handle uncertainty and improve exploration-exploitation trade-off during online phase.
AI.method: 1. Uses diffusion model prior knowledge to distill a fast-sampling diffusion actor policy and transition model in offline phase. 2. Employs Laplace approximation for uncertainty quantification. 3. Uses distance transition-state-shift detection. 4. Combines uncertainty estimates to improve exploration vs exploitation in online phase.
AI.result: Formally shows that the actor loss with Laplace approximation provides a proxy for principled epistemic uncertainty estimation. Empirically demonstrates improved online expected return over O2O-RL baselines across multiple settings and environments.
AI.conclusion: DUAL effectively addresses distribution shift challenges in offline-to-online RL by leveraging diffusion models with uncertainty quantification, resulting in more efficient online learning with better performance than existing approaches.

title: De-attribute to Forget for LLM Unlearning
summary: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns on the use of inappropriate data for training, which has led to a growing interest in LLM unlearning. Many existing LLM unlearning approaches rely on optimizing prediction loss(es), such as maximizing the loss on the forget set, but often face critical issues like over-forgetting and poor model utility. To address them, this paper novelly frames the optimization objective for LLM unlearning as one of zeroing out data attribution instead. In particular, we propose the first LLM unlearning framework based on data attribution rewards called DareU that performs reinforcement learning to update the LLM by reducing the attribution score of its generated responses (i.e., de-attributing) to the forget data owners. Empirical evaluation using an LLM classifier as an efficient approximation of attribution shows that DareU outperforms existing baselines by achieving effective unlearning while balancing forget quality and model utility well.

AI.tldr: DareU is a new LLM unlearning framework that uses reinforcement learning to reduce data attribution scores of model outputs to forget data owners, achieving better balance between unlearning effectiveness and model utility than loss-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM unlearning methods based on optimizing prediction losses often suffer from critical issues like over-forgetting (removing too much knowledge) and poor model utility (degrading performance on other tasks). There's growing interest in LLM unlearning due to concerns about using inappropriate data for training large language models.
AI.method: The paper frames LLM unlearning as zeroing out data attribution rather than optimizing prediction losses. It proposes DareU, the first LLM unlearning framework based on data attribution rewards. The approach uses reinforcement learning to update the LLM by reducing the attribution score (de-attributing) of its generated responses to the forget data owners. The method uses an LLM classifier as an efficient approximation of attribution.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows DareU outperforms existing baselines by achieving effective unlearning while balancing forget quality (ability to remove target data) and model utility (preserving overall performance) well. The method demonstrates superior performance compared to loss-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Framing LLM unlearning as a data attribution reduction problem rather than loss optimization is a promising direction. DareU provides an effective reinforcement learning-based solution that better maintains model utility while achieving the desired unlearning effects compared to previous approaches.

title: AbstainGNN: Teaching Graph Neural Networks to Abstain for Graph Classification
summary: Graph classification is a core task in graph data mining with widespread real-world applications. Recent advances in graph neural networks (GNNs) have led to substantial performance improvements for graph classification. However, existing GNNs are typically forced to make predictions even under high uncertainty or unknown conditions, resulting in unreliable decisions that can severely impact downstream tasks, particularly in safety-critical scenarios. To address this critical limitation, we propose AbstainGNN, a novel and theory-driven framework for graph classification with abstention, which enables GNNs to reject uncertain predictions instead of producing incorrect decisions. Specifically, AbstainGNN explicitly models both the predictive function and the abstention function, allowing for effective utilization of graph structural information. Moreover, unlike existing heuristic abstention methods, we theoretically characterize the trade-off between classification errors and rejection costs from a PAC-Bayesian generalization perspective, and derive a unified learning objective for model optimization. Guided by this theoretical insight, we further develop an efficient two-stage training strategy consisting of predictive function warm-start and abstention function calibration. Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets show that AbstainGNN outperforms existing abstention methods, achieving superior classification performance under the same rejection rates.

AI.tldr: AbstainGNN is a novel framework that enables graph neural networks to abstain from making uncertain predictions instead of producing potentially incorrect decisions in graph classification tasks, with theoretical guarantees and improved performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Graph neural networks (GNNs) currently make predictions even under high uncertainty or unknown conditions, leading to unreliable decisions that can severely impact downstream tasks, especially in safety-critical scenarios. There's a need for GNNs that can reject uncertain predictions rather than produce incorrect outputs.
AI.method: The proposed AbstainGNN framework explicitly models both a predictive function and an abstention function to leverage graph structural information effectively. It uses a PAC-Bayesian generalization perspective to theoretically characterize the trade-off between classification errors and rejection costs, leading to a unified learning objective. The method employs an efficient two-stage training strategy: predictive function warm-start followed by abstention function calibration.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five benchmark datasets show that AbstainGNN outperforms existing abstention methods, achieving superior classification performance under the same rejection rates.
AI.conclusion: AbstainGNN provides a theory-driven framework for reliable graph classification with abstention capability, addressing the critical limitation of existing GNNs that force predictions under uncertainty, making it particularly valuable for safety-critical applications where reliable decision-making is essential.

title: DEM: A Distilled Explanation Model for Interpretable Anomaly Detection in Physiological Sensor Networks
summary: Anomaly detection in physiological sensor data from Wireless Body Area Networks (WBANs) can be caused by sensor faults, network disruptions, or missing data, leading to false alarms. Hence, it demands both high predictive accuracy and clinically interpretable explanations. Existing approaches rely either on black-box models that achieve strong performance but offer no transparency, or on post-prediction explanation methods such as SHAP and LIME. In this paper, we propose the Distilled Explanation Model (DEM), a three-stage glass-box framework that distills the non-linear knowledge of a gradient boosting expert into an interpretable decision tree operating on residuals relative to a linear baseline, so that the explanation is not an approximation but the prediction itself. DEM introduces a novel distillation fidelity metric that quantifies how faithfully the explanation tree captures the expert model's non-linear contribution, providing a principled measure of explanation trustworthiness absent from prior interpretable models. Evaluated across four physiological datasets, including MIMIC-IV, WESAD, eICU, and an in-house SmartNet WBAN corpus, DEM achieves an AUC of 0.9964 on clinical contextual anomaly detection and 0.9047 on wearable stress detection while producing human-readable if-then rules at a controllable depth. Inference requires 0.17ms per 1000 samples, rendering DEM 1235x faster than SHAP-based post-hoc explanation and suitable for real-time physiological monitoring. Ablation studies confirm that the XGBoost distillation step provides measurable gains over naive residual fitting, and depth-sensitivity analysis demonstrates an explicit, user-controlled accuracy-interpretability trade-off unique to DEM among existing intrinsically interpretable models.

AI.tldr: DEM is a glass-box framework for anomaly detection in WBANs that distills gradient boosting knowledge into interpretable decision trees on residuals, offering high accuracy with human-readable explanations.
AI.motivation: Anomaly detection in physiological sensor data suffers from false alarms due to sensor faults, network issues, or missing data. Existing approaches either use black-box models with no transparency or post-prediction explanation methods like SHAP/LIME, which are approximations rather than the prediction itself.
AI.method: Three-stage glass-box framework: 1) Use a linear model as baseline, 2) Train gradient boosting expert (XGBoost) to capture non-linear patterns, 3) Distill expert's knowledge into an interpretable decision tree operating on residuals relative to the linear baseline. Introduces a novel distillation fidelity metric to measure how faithfully the tree captures expert's non-linear contributions.
AI.result: Achieves AUC of 0.9964 on clinical contextual anomaly detection and 0.9047 on wearable stress detection across four datasets (MIMIC-IV, WESAD, eICU, SmartNet WBAN). Produces human-readable if-then rules at controllable depth. Inference takes 0.17ms per 1000 samples (1235x faster than SHAP). Ablation studies show XGBoost distillation provides measurable gains over naive residual fitting.
AI.conclusion: DEM provides both high predictive accuracy and clinically interpretable explanations for anomaly detection in physiological data, with a unique user-controlled accuracy-interpretability trade-off that outperforms existing intrinsically interpretable models while being suitable for real-time monitoring.

title: Annealed Softmax Greedy in Many-Armed Bayesian Bandits
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) and group-based policy optimization methods such as GRPO update a stochastic policy by sampling multiple completions per prompt and increasing the policy's probability on those with higher reward, regularized by a KL penalty toward a reference policy. These updates do not include explicit mechanisms that track epistemic uncertainty. This paper studies a stylized explanation for why such uncertainty-agnostic updates can nevertheless be effective. We analyze an annealed softmax (Boltzmann) policy that selects actions according to a softmax of empirical mean rewards in a many-armed Bayesian Bernoulli bandit. Under a linear upper-tail condition on the prior (the $β=1$ case of $β$-regularity), which implies an abundance of near-optimal arms, we prove that annealed softmax greedy achieves Bayes regret $\tilde{O}(m + T/m)$, and in particular $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ when the number of arms scales as $m = Θ(\sqrt{T})$. This is the near-optimal Bayes regret rate in this regime, attained also by empirical-mean greedy. Under $β$-regularity, many arms maintain empirical means close to the optimum throughout learning, so when softmax samples an arm other than the empirically best, that arm tends to be another near-optimal one rather than a clearly inferior one. By contrast, with a small number of arms, the same kind of softmax policy can suffer linear regret. The result also provides a structural analogy to RLVR, where a base policy with a non-negligible probability of producing a correct completion plays the role of $β$-regularity.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why softmax-based policy optimization (like RLVR/GRPO) can be effective without explicit uncertainty tracking, showing that under certain conditions (β-regularity with abundant near-optimal arms), annealed softmax achieves near-optimal regret.
AI.motivation: Motivation: RLVR and GRPO methods use uncertainty-agnostic updates without explicit epistemic uncertainty tracking mechanisms, yet they are empirically effective. The paper seeks to provide theoretical understanding for why this works.
AI.method: Method: Analysis of an annealed softmax (Boltzmann) policy in a many-armed Bayesian Bernoulli bandit setting. The study examines regret under linear upper-tail conditions on prior distributions (β-regularity), proving theoretical bounds.
AI.result: Result: Under β=1 regularity (abundance of near-optimal arms), annealed softmax greedy achieves Bayes regret of \tilde{O}(m + T/m), and specifically \tilde{O}(√T) when m = Θ(√T). This matches empirical-mean greedy performance. The policy avoids large regret by sampling near-optimal arms when not picking the empirically best.
AI.conclusion: Conclusion: Softmax-based policies can be effective when there are many near-optimal options available (β-regularity condition). This provides structural analogy to RLVR, where base policies with decent probability of correct completions play the role of β-regularity.

title: Conformal Reliability: A New Evaluation Metric for Conditional Generation
summary: Conditional generative models have recently achieved remarkable success in various applications. However, a suitable metric for evaluating the reliability of these models, which takes into account their inherent uncertainty, is still lacking. Existing metrics, which typically assess a single output, may fail to capture the variability or potential risks in generation. In this paper, we propose a novel evaluation metric called reliability score based on conformal prediction, which measures the worst-case performance within the prediction set at a pre-specified confidence level. However, computing this score is challenging due to the high-dimensional nature of the output space and the nonconvexity of both the metric function and the prediction set. To efficiently compute this score, we introduce Conformal ReLiability (CReL), a framework that can (i) construct the prediction set with desired coverage; and (ii) accurately optimize the reliability score within the constructed prediction set. We provide theoretical results on coverage and demonstrate empirically that our method produces more informative prediction sets than existing approaches. Experiments on synthetic data and the image-to-text and text-to-image tasks further demonstrate the interpretability of our new metric, and the validity and effectiveness of our computational framework. Source code can be found at https://ggc29.github.io/CReL/.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CReL, a framework for evaluating conditional generative models using conformal prediction to measure worst-case performance within prediction sets at specified confidence levels, addressing limitations of existing single-output metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics for conditional generative models typically assess single outputs, failing to capture model uncertainty, variability, or potential risks in generation. There's a lack of suitable metrics that account for the inherent uncertainty in these models.
AI.method: Proposes Conformal ReLiability (CReL) framework: (1) constructs prediction sets with desired coverage using conformal prediction, and (2) efficiently computes the reliability score by optimizing the worst-case performance within these prediction sets, overcoming challenges from high-dimensional output space and nonconvexity.
AI.result: Theoretical coverage guarantees are provided, and empirical results show CReL produces more informative prediction sets than existing approaches. Experiments on synthetic data, image-to-text, and text-to-image tasks demonstrate the interpretability of the new metric and the validity/effectiveness of the computational framework.
AI.conclusion: CReL provides a novel, theoretically-grounded evaluation framework for conditional generative models that accounts for model uncertainty through conformal prediction, offering interpretable metrics and practical computational solutions for real-world applications.

title: Learning to Solve and Optimize by Evolving Code
comment: Preprint of a paper accepted to IJCAI26
summary: Combinatorial and optimization problems are fundamental to many industrial AI applications. Solving large-scale real-world instances of such problems typically requires careful problem formalization, specialized solvers, and expert-designed heuristics. Thus, experts need to specify not only what solutions are, but also how they are derived. By introducing the tool CHECKMATE, we show that algorithm generation via code evolution represents a paradigm shift by eliminating the need to formulate the how. CHECKMATE solely relies on the what. Specifically, a formal specification ensures solutions' correctness and enables systematic performance evaluation of the generated programs, while a natural language description guides the evolutionary process. The effectiveness of our method is demonstrated on selected problems from two industrial domains: configuration and scheduling. In all cases, the evolved algorithms consistently outperform state-of-the-art solvers. This underscores the potential of formal methods in guiding code evolution for automatically solving complex real-world problems.

AI.tldr: CHECKMATE introduces a paradigm shift in solving industrial AI problems by using code evolution to automatically generate algorithms from formal specifications (what) rather than requiring experts to specify the solution process (how), outperforming state-of-the-art solvers on configuration and scheduling problems.
AI.motivation: Current approaches to solving large-scale combinatorial and optimization problems require experts to specify both what solutions are and how they are derived (problem formalization, specialized solvers, expert-designed heuristics), which is time-consuming and requires specialized expertise.
AI.method: CHECKMATE uses algorithm generation via code evolution that eliminates the need to formulate "how" solutions are derived. It relies solely on formal specifications (what) for correctness verification and performance evaluation, while using natural language descriptions to guide the evolutionary process.
AI.result: The evolved algorithms consistently outperform state-of-the-art solvers on selected problems from two industrial domains: configuration and scheduling.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates the potential of formal methods in guiding code evolution for automatically solving complex real-world problems, representing a paradigm shift in how industrial AI problems can be solved.

title: IRIS: time-structured manifold projections
summary: High-dimensional biomedical data, such as cell-by-gene matrices, are increasingly generated temporally. However, Manifold Learning algorithms, like t-SNE and UMAP, cannot incorporate time-ordering in their layouts, obfuscating the dynamics of cell types or other classes. As a solution, we present IRIS, a new Manifold Learning algorithm that structures layouts both chronologically and by manifold topology. IRIS can visualize a wide range of dynamic biomedical data, including scRNA-seq, comparative metagenomics, and literature.

AI.tldr: IRIS is a new manifold learning algorithm that creates temporal-aware visualizations for dynamic biomedical data by structuring layouts both chronologically and by manifold topology.
AI.motivation: Current manifold learning algorithms like t-SNE and UMAP cannot incorporate time-ordering information in their visualizations, which makes it difficult to understand the dynamics and temporal evolution of cell types and other biological classes in high-dimensional biomedical data.
AI.method: IRIS is a new manifold learning algorithm designed to structure layouts both chronologically (incorporating time-ordering) and by manifold topology. This enables it to visualize temporal dynamics while preserving the underlying data structure.
AI.result: IRIS can visualize a wide range of dynamic biomedical data types including single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data, comparative metagenomics data, and literature data.
AI.conclusion: IRIS addresses the limitation of existing manifold learning tools by enabling temporal visualization of dynamic biomedical data, providing researchers with a better understanding of biological processes over time.

title: STEP: Learning STructured Embeddings for Progressive Time Series
summary: We present a novel method for learning interpretable representations of progressive time series, that is, data capturing irreversible state transitions such as degradation or task completion. Our approach uses a self-supervised contrastive objective to learn a low-dimensional latent space whose geometry is itself the interpretation: each observation becomes a point on a manifold anchored between two fixed orthogonal prototype vectors, and a trajectory becomes a path across that manifold. From this structure we read a latent compass, the polar coordinates (θ, r) of the latent vector, in which θ tracks the progression of the underlying state (e.g., from healthy to failed) and r identifies the active mode (e.g., the operating condition), without any proxy labels. We evaluate the approach against the state of the art on diverse domains, including industrial degradation, robotic tasks, and neural activity, validating three key capabilities: (1) end-state prediction, (2) multi-step forecasting, and (3) interpretable phase separation. Our method matches or improves over black-box counterparts on all of these while providing transparency about the underlying mechanisms. A simple linear regressor on top of the latent compass coordinates is competitive with deep architectures, direct quantitative evidence that the underlying state is encoded in a geometrically accessible form.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised method that learns interpretable latent representations for progressive time series using a contrastive objective, creating a geometrically meaningful manifold where polar coordinates track progression and identify operational modes without labels.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for progressive time series (irreversible state transitions like degradation or task completion) often produce black-box representations that lack interpretability about underlying mechanisms and progression stages.
AI.method: Uses self-supervised contrastive learning to create a low-dimensional latent space with orthogonal prototype vectors. Each observation becomes a point on a manifold between these prototypes, and trajectories become paths across this manifold. The geometry itself provides interpretation through polar coordinates (θ,r) that track progression (θ) and operational modes (r).
AI.result: Method matches or outperforms black-box alternatives on three key capabilities: (1) end-state prediction, (2) multi-step forecasting, and (3) interpretable phase separation. Works across industrial degradation, robotic tasks, and neural activity. A simple linear regressor on the latent coordinates competes with deep architectures, showing geometric accessibility.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides transparent, geometrically interpretable representations for progressive time series that maintain competitive performance while offering mechanistic insights into progression and mode identification without requiring proxy labels.

title: Non-destructive Identification of Oyster Species is possible from Hyperspectral Images with Machine Learning
summary: Differentiating between oyster species is important for developing new commercial oyster species suited to production systems and is critical for traceability in seafood supply chains. Common methods, such as DNA profiling, are destructive and time consuming. The possibility of using hyperspectral imaging (HSI) for discriminating between Black-Lip rock (BL) and Sydney rock (SR) oysters was investigated. Live BL and SR samples (N = 156) were scanned with a HSI camera (950-2515nm). Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis and Convolutional Neural Networks were trained with Monte Carlo Cross Validation to distinguish BL and SR oysters from the spectral reflectance of their left and rights valves. The PLS-DA model successfully distinguished between the species from both the left and right valves with a median test set classification accuracy of 100%, out performing the CNN with 83% and 96% respectively. Elemental and mineralogical composition in the surface and cross-section of oyster valves were measured with electron microscopy. Analysis of the right valve revealed a greater number of layers in BL compared to SR (4 vs 2). The concentrations of carbon and oxygen varied in the outer layer of the right valves, with BL being rich in carbon and SR being rich in oxygen. The variation in carbon and oxygen concentrations observed between BL and SR right valves may reflect differences in the relative abundance or composition of chitin and glycoproteins. This is supported by model-derived wavelength importance corresponding to vibrational modes of functional groups characteristic of these compounds. Transmittance analysis revealed that light was transmitted through the valves, around the valve edges, indicating that the spectral signatures may have been influenced by the other valve or the meat. Ultimately, the findings highlight an effective rapid, non-destructive methodology for oyster species.

AI.tldr: Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) can rapidly and non-destructively discriminate between Black-Lip and Sydney rock oyster species with high accuracy using Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis.
AI.motivation: Differentiating oyster species is important for developing commercial species and ensuring traceability in seafood supply chains, but current DNA-based methods are destructive and time-consuming.
AI.method: Used hyperspectral imaging (950-2515nm) on live oysters, applied Partial Least Square Discriminant Analysis (PLS-DA) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Monte Carlo Cross Validation, and performed elemental/mineralogical analysis with electron microscopy.
AI.result: PLS-DA achieved 100% classification accuracy for both left and right valves, outperforming CNN (83% and 96% respectively). BL oysters had more valve layers (4 vs 2) and higher carbon concentration, while SR oysters had higher oxygen concentration, suggesting differences in chitin/glycoprotein composition.
AI.conclusion: HSI with PLS-DA provides an effective, rapid, non-destructive methodology for oyster species discrimination, offering advantages over destructive DNA methods for commercial and traceability applications.

title: Trust-Region Behavior Blending for On-Policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on prefixes sampled from its own policy while matching a stronger teacher. This addresses the prefix mismatch of offline distillation, but early student rollouts can still be poor, placing teacher supervision on weak or low-quality prefixes. We propose Trust-Region behavior Blending (TRB), a warmup method that replaces the early rollout policy with the closest-to-teacher behavior policy inside a student-centered KL trust region, while keeping the per-prefix reverse-KL OPD loss unchanged. The KL budget is annealed to zero, so training returns to pure student rollouts after warmup. Across two math-reasoning distillation settings, TRB attains the strongest average among the compared methods.

AI.tldr: TRB is a warmup method for on-policy distillation that uses behavior policies within KL trust regions to improve early training by avoiding weak student rollouts.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses limitations of on-policy distillation (OPD) where early student rollouts are poor, placing teacher supervision on weak or low-quality prefixes. While OPD fixes the prefix mismatch problem of offline distillation, it still suffers from poor initial student behavior during warmup.
AI.method: The authors propose Trust-Region behavior Blending (TRB), which replaces early rollout policy with the closest-to-teacher behavior policy within a student-centered KL trust region. The KL budget is annealed to zero over time, gradually transitioning back to pure student rollouts after the warmup period while keeping the per-prefix reverse-KL OPD loss unchanged.
AI.result: TRB achieves the strongest average performance among compared methods across two math-reasoning distillation settings.
AI.conclusion: TRB effectively addresses the poor early rollout problem in on-policy distillation by intelligently blending behavior policies during warmup, leading to superior performance in reasoning tasks compared to existing methods.

title: Learning Permutation-invariant Macroscopic Dynamics
summary: Accurately modeling the macroscopic dynamics of high-dimensional microscopic systems is of broad interest across the sciences. Many data-driven approaches learn a low-dimensional latent state through an autoencoder trained for pointwise input reconstruction. These methods typically assume a fixed ordering of microscopic degrees of freedom in the input. However, in many settings, such as particle systems, the microscopic state is inherently unordered. This motivates an autoencoder framework that learns permutation-invariant latent representations. To this end, we adopt a permutation-invariant encoder and design the decoder to reconstruct the mass distribution centered at the observed points rather than per-sample reconstruction. We then jointly learn the macroscopic dynamics of the observables together with the latent states. We demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method across a range of microscopic settings, including learning the energy dynamics in interacting particle systems, predicting mixing dynamics in Lennard-Jones fluids, and modeling the stretching dynamics from video data of polymers moving in an elongational force field.

AI.tldr: An autoencoder framework that learns permutation-invariant latent representations for high-dimensional microscopic systems with unordered states (like particle systems), using a distribution reconstruction approach instead of pointwise reconstruction.
AI.motivation: Many data-driven approaches for modeling macroscopic dynamics of microscopic systems assume fixed ordering of microscopic degrees of freedom, but particle systems and similar settings have inherently unordered states, requiring permutation-invariant representations.
AI.method: Uses permutation-invariant encoder with decoder designed to reconstruct mass distribution centered at observed points rather than per-sample reconstruction. Jointly learns macroscopic dynamics of observables together with latent states.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness and robustness across multiple microscopic settings: learning energy dynamics in interacting particle systems, predicting mixing dynamics in Lennard-Jones fluids, and modeling stretching dynamics from video data of polymers in elongational force fields.
AI.conclusion: The proposed permutation-invariant autoencoder framework successfully handles unordered microscopic states and effectively learns macroscopic dynamics across diverse particle-based systems, offering a robust approach for systems where traditional autoencoders fail due to ordering assumptions.

title: What changes after deployment? A survey on On-device Learning in TinyML
summary: Machine learning models on microcontroller-class devices (TinyML) face a fundamental challenge: post-deployment distribution change undermines static models. On-device learning (ODL) addresses this by running the learning process directly on the device. The existing literature has not characterized how distribution change occurs or how different change types require different solutions. Approximately 70 ODL works are surveyed under one principle: the distribution change regime. The survey analyzes how different types of distribution change influence the applications addressable on-device, the hardware employed, and the structure of the solutions. A persistent gap between methodological benchmarks and real-world deployment scenarios is also identified.

AI.tldr: Survey of TinyML on-device learning research organized by the type of post-deployment data distribution change, showing how change types affect applications, hardware, and solutions.
AI.motivation: The fundamental challenge for TinyML is that post-deployment distribution change undermines static models. Current literature lacks a characterization of how distribution change occurs and how different types of change demand different ODL solutions.
AI.method: Survey of approximately 70 on-device learning (ODL) works. Analysis based on the type of distribution change and its impact on three key dimensions: addressable applications, employed hardware, and structure of ODL solutions.
AI.result: The survey provides a systematic characterization of distribution change regimes in TinyML. It analyzes how change types influence application domains, hardware choices, and solution structures. A significant gap between methodological benchmarks and real-world deployment scenarios is identified.
AI.conclusion: The survey establishes a unifying framework based on distribution change regimes to organize TinyML ODL research, identifies a benchmark-deployment gap, and provides guidance for designing effective ODL systems by considering application needs, hardware constraints, and change types.

title: EchoRL: Reinforcement Learning via Rollout Echoing
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards is an effective route for post-training to strengthen the reasoning capability of large language models. However, as training proceeds, the learning signal can collapse thus makes the training gain become marginal and ineffective. Specifically, a growing fraction of prompts' rollouts become advantage-degenerated: all the self-generated rollouts show verified-success, making the standard deviation over their rewards be zero; accordingly each rollout's advantage becomes degenerated (zero) as well. Given such rollouts' advantages, the policy-gradient for model optimization eventually vanishes, capping the training performance. We argue that some of these rollouts still contain valuable learning signals but unfortunately omitted with the existing RLVR methods. In this paper, inspired through analyzing the entropy pattern behind golden trajectories produced by external expert models, we propose EchoRL for better exploiting the advantage-degenerated rollouts to further improve the training performance. EchoRL is a lightweight module that first identifies an EchoClip from verified-success rollouts based on their step-level entropy values, and then feeds this clip back as an auxiliary supervision signal in the RL objective. Extensive experiments across 10 benchmarks, 5 LLM backbones, and 4 popular RLVR post-training methods demonstrate that EchoRL consistently improves RLVR post-training with minimal overhead.

AI.tldr: EchoRL is a lightweight module that improves reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) by better exploiting advantage-degenerated rollouts through entropy-based EchoClip identification and auxiliary supervision.
AI.motivation: RLVR methods for strengthening LLM reasoning capability suffer from vanishing learning signals as training progresses, because a growing fraction of rollouts become advantage-degenerated (all show verified-success with zero reward standard deviation). This causes policy-gradient to vanish and caps training performance, even though some of these rollouts still contain valuable learning signals that are currently omitted.
AI.method: EchoRL analyzes entropy patterns in golden trajectories from expert models, identifies an EchoClip from verified-success rollouts based on step-level entropy values, and feeds this clip back as auxiliary supervision in the RL objective to better exploit advantage-degenerated rollouts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 10 benchmarks, 5 LLM backbones, and 4 popular RLVR post-training methods demonstrate that EchoRL consistently improves RLVR post-training performance with minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: EchoRL effectively addresses the vanishing gradient problem in RLVR by exploiting advantage-degenerated rollouts through entropy-based supervision, leading to consistent performance improvements across various benchmarks and model architectures with low computational cost.

title: Cross-Layer Subspace Coupling for LLM Compression: A Unifying Framework and Its Empirical Limits
summary: Recent SVD based compression methods for large language models like SVD LLM and Basis Sharing can be unified under one optimization problem. While mathematical proofs and tests on Pythia models show this unified approach improves weight reconstruction error by up to 46% percent it fails in practical tasks. Downstream metrics like perplexity and accuracy severely degrade compared to standard per layer SVD LLM. The authors explain this failure mechanistically. Although the bundle method mathematically couples adjacent layers the transformer residual stream actually decouples them during forward passes. Thus per layer optimality matters more than joint cross layer optimization. The paper concludes that weight space reconstruction is a flawed objective for cross layer compression and future methods must focus on per layer activation reconstruction instead.

AI.tldr: Joint cross-layer SVD compression mathematically improves weight reconstruction but fails practically; per-layer optimization works better because transformer residual streams decouple layers during forward passes.
AI.motivation: To unify recent SVD-based compression methods for large language models and understand why mathematically superior joint optimization methods fail in practical tasks despite improved weight reconstruction.
AI.method: Unifying SVD-based compression methods (SVD LLM and Basis Sharing) under a single optimization problem, analyzing weight reconstruction error and downstream performance metrics like perplexity and accuracy.
AI.result: While the unified approach reduces weight reconstruction error by up to 46%, it severely degrades downstream performance metrics (perplexity and accuracy) compared to standard per-layer SVD LLM.
AI.conclusion: Weight space reconstruction is a flawed objective for cross-layer compression; future methods should focus on per-layer activation reconstruction instead.

title: Learning Cardiac Latent Representations in Vectorcardiogram Space
summary: Electrocardiography (ECG) is a cornerstone of cardiac assessment, making the learning of informative ECG representations fundamental to tasks ranging from disease diagnosis to clinical report generation. However, existing methods operate almost exclusively in the observable ECG signal space. In practice, the standard twelve-lead ECG represents multiple projections of the same underlying cardiac electrical activity from different spatial orientations. Therefore, representation learning in the ECG space inevitably introduces substantial redundancy, which may lead to spurious correlations and increased risk of overfitting. To address this and motivated by the Frank vectorcardiogram (VCG) model, we propose learning a unified latent representation of cardiac electrical activity directly in the VCG space. We introduce LVCG, the first general self-supervised representation learning framework designed to operate in this physically grounded latent space. By learning view-invariant latent VCG representations rather than lead-specific artifacts, VCG minimizes redundancy and improves generalization. LVCG generally outperforms ECG-space baselines across tasks, demonstrating enhanced robustness and generalization, especially in domain shift settings.

AI.tldr: LVCG is a self-supervised framework that learns cardiac representations in vectorcardiogram (VCG) space rather than traditional ECG space, reducing redundancy and improving generalization across ECG analysis tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard twelve-lead ECG represents multiple projections of the same underlying cardiac electrical activity, leading to substantial redundancy when learning representations in ECG space. This redundancy can cause spurious correlations and increased risk of overfitting. The Frank vectorcardiogram (VCG) model provides a more physically grounded representation of cardiac electrical activity.
AI.method: Proposes LVCG, a general self-supervised representation learning framework that operates in the VCG space rather than traditional ECG signal space. The approach learns view-invariant latent VCG representations that minimize lead-specific artifacts and redundancy.
AI.result: LVCG generally outperforms ECG-space baselines across various tasks, demonstrating enhanced robustness and generalization, particularly in domain shift settings. The approach shows improved performance compared to traditional ECG representation learning methods.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging the VCG space's physically grounded representation, LVCG reduces redundancy, minimizes spurious correlations, and enables more robust and generalizable ECG analysis across various clinical applications.

title: CoMem: Context Management with A Decoupled Long-Context Model
summary: Context management enables agentic models to solve long-horizon tasks through iterative summarization of previous interaction histories. However, this process typically incurs substantial decoding overhead for the extra summarization tokens, which significantly affect the end-to-end response latency at deployment. In this paper, we introduce CoMem, a novel framework that decouples memory management from the primary agent workflow, enabling these processes to execute in parallel. We propose a $k$-step-off asynchronous pipeline that overlaps the memory model's summarization with the agent's inference, effectively masking the latency of context processing. To ensure robustness under this asynchronous setting, we introduce a reward-driven training strategy that aligns the memory model to capture sufficient statistics for the agent's decision-making. Theoretical analysis confirms that CoMem offers a superior efficiency-effectiveness trade-off compared to coupled architectures. Our extensive experimental results on SWE-Bench-Verified show that CoMem provides 1.4x latency improvements upon vanilla long-context solutions while preserving most of the performance. Furthermore, we demonstrate that these latency gains scale favorably with increased system throughput, offering a modular path forward for the independent optimization of agent reasoning and memory compression.

AI.tldr: CoMem decouples memory management from agent workflows using asynchronous pipeline, reducing latency 1.4x while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Context management in agentic models involves substantial decoding overhead from iterative summarization of interaction histories, which significantly increases end-to-end response latency during deployment.
AI.method:
1. Decouples memory management from primary agent workflow using a k-step-off asynchronous pipeline 
2. Overlaps memory model summarization with agent's inference to mask context processing latency 
3. Implements reward-driven training strategy to align memory model for robust decision-making
AI.result: Experimental results on SWE-Bench-Verified show 1.4x latency improvements compared to vanilla long-context solutions while preserving most performance. Latency gains scale favorably with increased system throughput.
AI.conclusion: CoMem introduces a novel asynchronous framework that decouples memory management from agent workflows, enabling parallel execution and latency masking while preserving performance. It offers a modular approach for independently optimizing agent reasoning and memory compression.

title: Why Linear Recurrent Memory Works in Partially Observable Reinforcement Learning
summary: The family of linear recurrent neural networks has shown strong performance as recurrent memory units in partially observable reinforcement learning. We provide a theoretical justification for their empirical effectiveness by constructing and studying two linear filters: (i) the first exactly reproduces the pre-softmax logits of the belief vector in a hidden Markov model (HMM) under a deterministic transition matrix, thereby serving as a sufficient statistic for optimal policy learning, (ii) the second achieves vanishing state-decoding error under a nearly deterministic transition matrix, thus reducing state ambiguity to near zero. The results extend to action-controlled HMMs, where the corresponding linear filters become time-varying with action-dependent dynamics. We illustrate our main results through numerical experiments and further show that the constructed linear filter serves as a strong feature extractor in a small reinforcement learning game.

AI.tldr: Linear RNNs theoretically proven effective for partially observable RL by constructing linear filters that recover HMM belief vectors and reduce state ambiguity.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to provide theoretical justification for the empirical effectiveness of linear recurrent neural networks as recurrent memory units in partially observable reinforcement learning, addressing the gap between their practical success and theoretical understanding.
AI.method: The authors construct and study two linear filters: (1) one that exactly reproduces the pre-softmax logits of the belief vector in HMMs with deterministic transitions, and (2) another that achieves vanishing state-decoding error under nearly deterministic transition matrices. These analyses extend to action-controlled HMMs with time-varying, action-dependent dynamics.
AI.result: The constructed linear filters: (1) serve as sufficient statistics for optimal policy learning in HMMs with deterministic transitions, and (2) reduce state ambiguity to near zero under nearly deterministic transition matrices. Numerical experiments demonstrate their effectiveness, and the filter serves as a strong feature extractor in reinforcement learning tasks.
AI.conclusion: This paper provides theoretical justification for linear recurrent neural networks as recurrent memory units in reinforcement learning, showing they can effectively learn hidden Markov models and serve as strong feature extractors.

title: A Lecture Note on Offline RL and IRL, Part II: Foundations of Inverse Reinforcement Learning and Dynamic Discrete Choice Models
summary: In the forward reinforcement-learning problem, the reward is fixed and known; the learner is asked to find a good policy or value function. Here we turn the question around. Given offline data generated by an expert, can we recover the reward the expert was optimizing? This is the inverse reinforcement learning problem, and remarkably, two communities, structural econometricians studying dynamic discrete choice (DDC) and machine learners studying entropy-regularized IRL, have been working on exactly the same probabilistic model under different names. We begin by proving their equivalence. We then develop the classical identification result of Magnac and Thesmar and the classical computational paradigms that grew out of it: Rust's nested fixed-point algorithm, the conditional-choice-probability approach of Hotz and Miller, and the two temporal-difference approaches of Adusumilli and Eckardt: linear semi-gradient TD and approximate value iteration. Each route has its limits: dimensionality, transition-kernel estimation, the deadly triad, or projected fixed-point bias. We then walk through the modern ML/IRL strand: adversarial IRL, occupancy matching, IQ-Learn, and offline ML-IRL, deriving each method's actual objective and stating precisely what it does and does not identify. We close with the empirical-risk-minimization framework of Kang et al., which yields a gradient-based estimator for offline IRL/DDC.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes equivalence between structural econometrics (dynamic discrete choice) and machine learning (inverse reinforcement learning) approaches to recovering reward functions from expert data, surveys classical and modern methods, and presents an empirical-risk-minimization framework for gradient-based estimation.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to bridge two separate research communities - structural econometricians studying dynamic discrete choice (DDC) and machine learners studying inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) - that have been working on the same probabilistic model without recognizing their equivalence. The motivation is to unify these approaches and systematically compare their methodological developments.
AI.method: The paper first proves the equivalence between DDC and entropy-regularized IRL models. It then systematically surveys classical methods including: 1) identification results (Magnac and Thesmar), 2) computational paradigms (Rust's nested fixed-point, Hotz-Miller conditional-choice-probability, Adusumilli-Eckardt TD approaches), and 3) modern ML/IRL methods (adversarial IRL, occupancy matching, IQ-Learn, offline ML-IRL). Finally presents the empirical-risk-minimization framework of Kang et al.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that structural econometrics (DDC) and machine learning (entropy-regularized IRL) are mathematically equivalent formulations of the same problem. It provides a comprehensive mapping of classical econometric approaches to modern ML methods, highlighting limitations of each approach (dimensionality issues, transition-kernel estimation challenges, the deadly triad, projected fixed-point bias).
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully unifies two previously separate research strands, provides a comprehensive survey of classical and modern approaches to reward recovery from expert data, and establishes the empirical-risk-minimization framework as a gradient-based estimator that bridges both traditions, enabling more efficient and theoretically grounded solutions to the inverse reinforcement learning/dynamic discrete choice problem.

title: The Terminal Representation in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Representation learning is a powerful tool for spatio-temporal abstraction within reinforcement learning (RL). Two well established approaches are through the successor representation (SR) and the default representation (DR). The SR encodes states by the future trajectories they induce, capturing information flow decoupled from reward. The DR builds on this by weighting trajectories with reward, integrating credit-assignment structure into the representation. Eigenvectors of both representations have been used to support a range of downstream tasks -- including option discovery, reward shaping, transfer learning, and exploration. We introduce a structurally distinct formulation: the terminal representation (TR). The TR encodes reward-weighted trajectories similarly to the DR, but can be learned as a lower-dimensionality object, and can be used directly for the mentioned applications without eigenvector computations. Eigendecomposition also imposes the assumption of symmetric transition dynamics, which the TR can bypass. In this work we develop the theoretical foundations of the TR: its derivation, convergence of two learning algorithms, its use for zero-shot compositionality, and equivalences between alternative reward formulations. We further show the TR is embedded in the top DR eigenvector, allowing it to capture the same underlying knowledge without eigendecomposition. Additionally, we provide empirical evidence of the TR as a viable alternative to existing representations in subsidiary applications, while requiring less computational overhead to learn, store, and use.

AI.tldr: The Terminal Representation (TR) is a novel reinforcement learning representation that encodes reward-weighted trajectories like Default Representations but with lower dimensionality and no need for eigendecomposition, making it more computationally efficient while preserving similar capabilities.
AI.motivation: Current spatio-temporal representations like Successor Representations (SR) and Default Representations (DR) require eigendecomposition, which imposes computational overhead and assumes symmetric transition dynamics. There's a need for a more efficient representation that can capture similar knowledge without these limitations.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Terminal Representation (TR) as a structurally distinct formulation that encodes reward-weighted trajectories similarly to DR. They develop theoretical foundations including derivation, convergence proofs for two learning algorithms, and show how TR can be used for zero-shot compositionality. They also demonstrate that TR is embedded in the top DR eigenvector, allowing it to bypass eigendecomposition requirements.
AI.result: Theoretical results show TR can be learned as a lower-dimensionality object and used directly for downstream applications without eigenvector computations. Empirical evidence demonstrates TR as a viable alternative to existing representations in various applications while requiring less computational overhead for learning, storage, and use.
AI.conclusion: TR offers a computationally efficient alternative to existing spatio-temporal representations in RL, capturing similar knowledge without requiring eigendecomposition or assuming symmetric transition dynamics, making it practical for various downstream applications including option discovery, reward shaping, transfer learning, and exploration.

title: ForecastCompass: Guiding Agentic Forecasting with Adaptive Factor Memory
summary: Agentic forecasting is important for decision-making in dynamic environments, but it remains challenging because agents must reason from incomplete, time-limited evidence and produce calibrated probabilities before outcomes are resolved. Memory provides a natural mechanism for transferring experience from resolved forecasts to future prediction tasks. However, existing agent-memory methods are not tailored to forecasting, as they typically store past interactions, reflections, or factual associations without explicitly representing reusable predictive factors or calibration knowledge. We propose ForecastCompass (FoCo), an adaptive factor-based memory framework for agentic forecasting. FoCo organizes forecasting experience with a hierarchical forecasting-task taxonomy, enabling retrieval task-relevant forecasting knowledge. It maintains two complementary memory components: factor memory, which captures reusable predictive dimensions, and reasoning memory, which encodes probability updating, uncertainty handling, and calibration principles. Using retrospective analyses as learning signals, FoCo iteratively revises memory through a verbalized memory-revision procedure, enabling the agent to accumulate transferable forecasting knowledge over time. Experiments on Prophet Arena and FutureX with GPT-5-mini and Gemini-2.5-Flash show that FoCo improves both probabilistic accuracy and calibration.

AI.tldr: FoCo is a novel memory framework that helps AI agents improve forecasting by storing and reusing predictive factors and calibration knowledge through a hierarchical taxonomy and verbalized memory revision.
AI.motivation: Current agent-memory methods are inadequate for forecasting tasks because they store past interactions, reflections, or factual associations but lack explicit representation of reusable predictive factors or calibration knowledge needed for probabilistic forecasting in dynamic environments.
AI.method: Proposes ForecastCompass (FoCo) - an adaptive factor-based memory framework with: 1) hierarchical forecasting-task taxonomy for organizing experience, 2) factor memory for reusable predictive dimensions, 3) reasoning memory for probability updating and calibration principles, and 4) verbalized memory-revision procedure using retrospective analyses as learning signals.
AI.result: Experiments on Prophet Arena and FutureX datasets using GPT-5-mini and Gemini-2.5-Flash show that FoCo improves both probabilistic accuracy and calibration compared to existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: FoCo successfully addresses the limitations of existing agent-memory methods for forecasting by providing a specialized framework that accumulates transferable forecasting knowledge over time, leading to better calibrated probabilistic predictions in dynamic environments.

title: Inconsistency-Aware Minimization: Improving Generalization with Unlabeled Data
summary: Estimating the generalization gap and developing optimization methods that improve generalization are crucial for deep learning models, for both theoretical understanding and practical applications. Leveraging unlabeled data for these purposes offers significant advantages in real-world scenarios. This paper introduces a novel generalization measure, local inconsistency, derived from an information-geometric perspective on the parameter space of neural networks. A key feature of local inconsistency is that it can be computed without explicit labels. We establish theoretical underpinnings by connecting local inconsistency to the Fisher information matrix and the loss Hessian. Empirically, we demonstrate that local inconsistency correlates with the generalization gap. Based on these findings, we propose Inconsistency-Aware Minimization (IAM), which incorporates local inconsistency into the training objective. We demonstrate that in standard supervised learning settings, IAM enhances generalization, achieving performance comparable to that of existing methods such as Sharpness-Aware Minimization. Furthermore, IAM exhibits efficacy in semi- and self-supervised learning scenarios, where the local inconsistency is computed from unlabeled data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel label-free generalization measure called "local inconsistency" from an information-geometric perspective, and proposes Inconsistency-Aware Minimization (IAM) training that incorporates this measure to improve generalization in various learning settings.
AI.motivation: Estimating generalization gap and developing methods to improve generalization are crucial for deep learning models. Leveraging unlabeled data for these purposes offers practical advantages in real-world scenarios where labeled data is scarce or expensive.
AI.method: Proposes "local inconsistency" - a novel generalization measure derived from information-geometric analysis of neural network parameter space. This measure can be computed without explicit labels. Then introduces Inconsistency-Aware Minimization (IAM) which incorporates local inconsistency into the training objective to improve generalization.
AI.result: Empirical demonstration that local inconsistency correlates with generalization gap. IAM enhances generalization in standard supervised learning, achieving comparable performance to Sharpness-Aware Minimization. IAM also shows efficacy in semi- and self-supervised learning scenarios where local inconsistency is computed from unlabeled data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed local inconsistency measure provides a theoretically grounded, label-free way to estimate generalization, and the IAM training method effectively leverages this measure to improve model generalization across various learning paradigms, particularly valuable in semi-supervised and self-supervised contexts.

title: dashi: A Python library for Dataset Shift Characterization to Support Trustworthy AI Development and Deployment
summary: The Artificial Intelligence (AI) life cycle requires a thorough understanding of the underlying data dynamics for robust, safe and cost-effective AI development and use. Dataset shifts are defined as changes between train and test data distributions. Whether occurring over time (temporal) or across different sites (multi-source), they can severely degrade model performance and compromise data quality. This is particularly important in health AI, where the safety and fundamental rights of patients can be severely affected by uncontrolled shifts both at training and operational stages. While the theoretical foundations of covariate, prior, and concept shifts are well established, there is a lack of accessible and comprehensive software tools to perform their analysis. We introduce dashi, an open-source Python library designed for the exploration, quantification, and characterization of dataset shifts. dashi provides a dual approach: an unsupervised approach that leverages information geometry and non-parametric statistical manifolds to data variability characterization and analysis (e.g., Information Geometric Temporal plots and Multi-Source Variability metrics like Global Probabilistic Deviation and Source Probabilistic Outlyingness), and a supervised approach that quantifies and characterizes model performance degradation. Both unsupervised and supervised approaches work across user-defined temporal and domain/source batches. We demonstrate the utility of dashi on three simulated and real-world health AI case studies on gestational diabetes mellitus, COVID-19 and emergency medical dispatch. By providing interactive visual analytics and variability metrics, dashi supports trustworthiness of AI life cycle stages enabling robust and safe machine learning pipelines through the assessment of data coherence and AI performance.

AI.tldr: dashi is an open-source Python library for detecting and analyzing dataset shifts in AI pipelines, offering both unsupervised (information geometry-based) and supervised approaches to quantify distribution changes between training and test data.
AI.motivation: Dataset shifts between training and test data can severely degrade AI model performance and compromise data quality, especially in critical domains like healthcare where patient safety and rights are at stake. While theoretical foundations exist, there's a lack of accessible software tools to analyze these shifts systematically.
AI.method: The authors developed dashi with two complementary approaches: 1) Unsupervised method using information geometry and non-parametric statistical manifolds for data variability characterization (IGT plots, Global Probabilistic Deviation, Source Probabilistic Outlyingness), and 2) Supervised method for quantifying model performance degradation. Both work across temporal and domain/source batches.
AI.result: dashi provides interactive visual analytics and variability metrics for exploring, quantifying, and characterizing dataset shifts. The library's utility was demonstrated on three health AI case studies: gestational diabetes mellitus, COVID-19, and emergency medical dispatch.
AI.conclusion: dashi enables robust and safe AI development by supporting trustworthiness assessment throughout the AI lifecycle through comprehensive dataset shift analysis, helping maintain data coherence and model performance integrity in critical applications like healthcare.

title: GlucoFM: A Dual-Stream Foundation Model for Continuous Glucose Monitoring
summary: Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) provides a dense view of daily metabolic physiology, yet existing generic time-series and CGM-specific foundation models often encode glucose traces as entangled single-stream sequences, leaving the distinct temporal structure of glycemic dynamics only implicitly modeled.
