AI.conclusion: The paper provides a unified toolkit for understanding and designing efficient and expressive token mixers across different model families, offering principled ways to trade off runtime for expressivity while maintaining theoretical guarantees.

title: A Unifying View of Variational Generative Wasserstein Flows
comment: Accepted as a spotlight at ICML2026
summary: Many modern generative models can be viewed as minimizing divergences between probability distributions, yet they rely on different algorithmic and geometric principles. Wasserstein gradient flows provide a continuous-time formulation for optimizing over distributions, and can be approximated through their implicit discretization via the Jordan-Kinderlehrer-Otto (JKO) scheme. In this work, we present a unified theoretical framework for generative modeling based on Wasserstein gradient flows, which we refer to as Generative Wasserstein Flows (GWF). We show that a broad class of existing methods can be derived as instances of parametric JKO schemes for $f$-divergence objectives, and we establish equivalences between several recently proposed algorithms. We extend this framework beyond f-divergence to Integral Probability Metrics and squared Maximum Mean Discrepancy, deriving new JKO-based generative algorithms, and clarifying their connections with GANs. We study empirically the impact of the JKO regularization for a wide set of objectives. Finally, we analyze parametric Wasserstein flows, where the dynamics are restricted to distributions induced by parametrized maps.

AI.tldr: A unified framework called Generative Wasserstein Flows connects existing generative models through Wasserstein gradient flows and the JKO scheme, revealing equivalences between algorithms and enabling new derivations.
AI.motivation: Many modern generative models minimize divergences between distributions but rely on different algorithmic principles. The paper aims to provide a unified theoretical framework connecting these disparate approaches through the mathematical foundation of Wasserstein gradient flows.
AI.method: Uses Wasserstein gradient flows and their implicit discretization via the JKO scheme to create a unified framework for generative modeling. Extends beyond f-divergences to Integral Probability Metrics and squared Maximum Mean Discrepancy, deriving new JKO-based algorithms. Studies parametric implementations where dynamics are restricted to parametrized distributions.
AI.result: Shows that a broad class of existing generative methods can be derived as instances of parametric JKO schemes for f-divergence objectives, establishes equivalences between several recently proposed algorithms, derives new JKO-based generative algorithms, and clarifies connections with GANs. Empirical studies analyze the impact of JKO regularization.
AI.conclusion: This work presents a unified theoretical framework called Generative Wasserstein Flows (GWF) that connects many existing generative models through the lens of Wasserstein gradient flows and the JKO scheme, establishing equivalences between algorithms, deriving new JKO-based methods, and analyzing parametric implementations.

title: Softsign: Smooth Sign in Your Optimizer For Better Parameter Heterogeneity Handling
summary: Sign-based and LMO-inspired optimizers have recently attracted substantial attention in deep learning due to their strong performance and low memory footprint. However, their fixed-magnitude updates can hurt terminal convergence: they decouple update mechanisms from gradient magnitudes and fail to account for parameter heterogeneity, often leading to oscillation rather than convergence. We propose SoftSignum, a smooth relaxation of sign-based optimization that replaces the hard sign map with a temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation, enabling a parameter-wise transition from sign-like updates to magnitude-sensitive SGD-like steps. We complement it with an adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule and extend the same principle to matrix-valued optimizers, obtaining SoftMuon. We also develop a generalized geometry-relaxation framework based on strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates, proving convergence in stochastic non-convex setting. Experiments on diverse deep learning tasks, including LLM pretraining, show that SoftSignum and SoftMuon consistently improve over their hard sign-based counterparts and standard AdamW.

AI.tldr: SoftSignum improves sign-based optimizers by replacing hard sign function with smooth relaxation and adaptive temperature, enabling magnitude-sensitive updates while maintaining low memory footprint
AI.motivation: Sign-based optimizers have low memory footprint but suffer from fixed-magnitude updates that decouple from gradient magnitudes, ignore parameter heterogeneity, and cause oscillation rather than convergence in terminal phases
AI.method: 1) Replace hard sign function with temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation, 2) Use adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule, 3) Extend to matrix-valued optimizers (SoftMuon), 4) Develop generalized geometry-relaxation framework based on strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates
AI.result: Experiments on diverse deep learning tasks including LLM pretraining show consistent improvements over hard sign-based counterparts and standard AdamW
AI.conclusion: SoftSignum and SoftMuon effectively address the limitations of sign-based optimizers by introducing smooth relaxations that enable magnitude-sensitive updates while maintaining low memory requirements. They consistently outperform both hard sign-based methods and standard AdamW across various deep learning tasks.

title: Constrained Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning with Max-Min Criterion
summary: Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning (MORL) extends standard RL by optimizing policies with respect to multiple, often conflicting, objectives. While max-min MORL has emerged as an effective approach for promoting fairness, its applicability remains limited, particularly when constraints must be incorporated. In this paper, we propose a MORL framework that integrates the max-min criterion with explicit constraint satisfaction. We establish a theoretical foundation for the proposed framework and validate the resulting algorithm through convergence analysis and experiments in tabular settings. We further demonstrate the practical relevance of our approach in simulated building thermal control, multi-objective locomotion control, and greenhouse-gas-emission-aware traffic management. Across these domains, our method effectively balances fairness and constraint satisfaction in multi-objective decision-making.

AI.tldr: A MORL framework combining max-min fairness with constraint satisfaction, validated theoretically and applied to real-world control problems.
AI.motivation: Max-min MORL is effective for fairness but has limited applicability when constraints must be incorporated, requiring a framework that integrates both fairness and constraint satisfaction.
AI.method: A MORL framework integrating max-min criterion with explicit constraint satisfaction, supported by theoretical foundation and validated through convergence analysis in tabular settings.
AI.result: The framework effectively balances fairness and constraint satisfaction in three domains: building thermal control, locomotion control, and traffic management with GHG emissions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MORL framework with max-min criterion and constraint satisfaction successfully balances fairness and constraint requirements across diverse real-world applications.

title: Fixed Universal Transformers
summary: We introduce \emph{universal transformers}: fixed transformers that can simulate any transformer in a given class via a suitable input embedding. Analogous to a universal Turing machine, the input embedding encodes a description of the target model while all internal parameters remain fixed. We provide explicit sparse constructions achieving universality when the embedding dimension is sufficiently large, and further show that universality is generic: randomly initialized transformers are universal almost surely, which aligns with recent empirical results of Zhong and Andreas (2024). We empirically validate our theory on the algorithmic tasks of parenthesis balancing and multi-hop reasoning. Our results suggest that much of a transformer's expressive power may reside in its input representation rather than its learned weights.

AI.tldr: Universal transformers are fixed transformers that can simulate any transformer via input embedding, with constructions showing universality is generic and empirically validated.
AI.motivation: Prove that transformers can be universal in the sense that fixed transformers can simulate any transformer in a given class via suitable input embedding, analogous to universal Turing machines.
AI.method: Provide explicit sparse constructions for universal transformers and show that randomly initialized transformers are universal almost surely. Validate theory empirically on algorithmic tasks.
AI.result: Explicit sparse constructions achieve universality when embedding dimension is sufficiently large. Randomly initialized transformers are universal almost surely, aligning with empirical findings.
AI.conclusion: Much of a transformer's expressive power may reside in its input representation rather than its learned weights, suggesting universality is a generic property.

title: DG-CoLearn: An Efficient Collaborative Learning Framework for Dynamic Graphs
summary: Dynamic graph learning (DGL) is essential for modelling evolving graph data, but existing methods suffer from significant computational overhead due to repeated full-snapshot retraining and are not well-suited for collaborative settings with partitioned data. In realistic graph systems, cross-partition edges are unavoidable, but direct sharing of graph structure between clients may violate privacy constraints. We propose DG-CoLearn, a client-oblivious collaborative dynamic graph learning framework built on incremental graph snapshot processing, which focuses computation on graph regions affected by temporal updates while preserving historical information through temporal modelling. This incremental design is consistently applied across the entire graph processing pipeline, including a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism to enable accurate multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client structural information. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DG-CoLearn achieves up to 33.8$\times$ speedup in training time and 27.4$\times$ reduction in communication overhead, while consistently improving predictive performance on both node classification (up to 13.36% F1 improvement) and link prediction (up to 8.27% MAP improvement) tasks. These results highlight the effectiveness of DG-CoLearn in bridging efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client structural privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning.

AI.tldr: DG-CoLearn is a collaborative dynamic graph learning framework that uses incremental snapshot processing and embedding exchange to achieve efficient training while preserving privacy across partitioned graph data.
AI.motivation: Existing dynamic graph learning methods suffer from high computational overhead due to repeated full-snapshot retraining and are not suitable for collaborative settings with partitioned data, where cross-partition edges exist but sharing graph structure violates privacy constraints.
AI.method: DG-CoLearn uses incremental graph snapshot processing to focus computation on graph regions affected by temporal updates, preserves historical information through temporal modeling, and employs a server-mediated embedding exchange mechanism for accurate multi-hop message passing without exposing raw cross-client structural information.
AI.result: DG-CoLearn achieves up to 33.8x training speedup, 27.4x reduction in communication overhead, and improves predictive performance by up to 13.36% F1 for node classification and 8.27% MAP for link prediction.
AI.conclusion: DG-CoLearn effectively bridges efficiency, scalability, and client-to-client structural privacy in collaborative dynamic graph learning, demonstrating substantial performance improvements over traditional methods.

title: Flow map learning in nonlinear vector autoregressive models: influence of the feature-library structure on the training error
summary: Time series forecasting often requires learning nonlinear and time-delayed dependencies. A paradigmatic class of forecasting models are nonlinear vector autoregressive processes (NVAR), also known as next-generation reservoir computers (NG-RCs). These models approximate the Koopman operator on the space spanned by their explicit feature library. We consider the identifiability problem for learning Markovian nonlinear dynamical systems and show that the training error as a function of time resolution follows characteristic (pre-)asymptotic scaling laws. These laws depend on whether the feature library can represent the early Lie-series coefficients of the flow map (propagator) exactly or merely approximately. For dynamical systems governed by polynomial vector fields, we demonstrate the mechanism for NVAR/NG-RC models with monomial and Fourier feature libraries. We determine the dependence of the training error on the temporal resolution, the involved nonlinear degree, and the number of delay terms. While delay terms reduce the optimal one-step training error, they improve long-horizon forecasts only when the library provides sufficient nonlinearity. Thus, small training error coexists with weak generalization as the model class is mismatched to the true data-generating process. Numerical experiments on various chaotic dynamical systems confirm the theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: NVAR/NG-RC models for forecasting nonlinear dynamical systems show scaling laws for training error based on time resolution, nonlinear degree, and delay terms. Delay terms help one-step error but need sufficient library nonlinearity for long-term forecasts.
AI.motivation: Time series forecasting requires learning nonlinear and time-delayed dependencies. Nonlinear vector autoregressive processes (NVAR) and next-generation reservoir computers (NG-RCs) approximate the Koopman operator but face identifiability challenges. Understanding the scaling laws of training error with temporal resolution is crucial for assessing model generalization capabilities.
AI.method: The study considers identifiability problems for learning Markovian nonlinear dynamical systems using NVAR/NG-RC models. For polynomial vector fields, the analysis focuses on monomial and Fourier feature libraries to understand how training error scales with temporal resolution, nonlinear degree, and number of delay terms. The approach involves theoretical analysis of how feature libraries represent Lie-series coefficients of flow maps.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis reveals characteristic (pre-)asymptotic scaling laws for training error as a function of time resolution. These laws depend on whether feature libraries can exactly or approximately represent early Lie-series coefficients. Delay terms reduce optimal one-step training error but only improve long-horizon forecasts when the library provides sufficient nonlinearity.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that for polynomial dynamical systems, NVAR/NG-RC models exhibit characteristic scaling laws for training error based on temporal resolution, nonlinear degree, and delay terms. While delay terms reduce one-step training error, they only improve long-horizon forecasts when the feature library provides sufficient nonlinearity. Numerical experiments confirm that small training error can coexist with weak generalization when the model class is mismatched to the true data-generating process.

title: DRIFT: Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning for Efficient Multi-Turn Optimization
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in multi-turn interactive settings where users or environments can iteratively provide lightweight feedback. Unfortunately, optimizing such behavior presents a sharp dilemma in practice: online reinforcement learning is able to effectively address multi-turn dynamics but is prohibitively expensive due to the cost of generating full correction trajectories at every update, whereas offline supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is efficient but suffers from distribution shift and behavioral collapse. To this end, we novelly propose DRIFT (Decoupled Rollouts and Importance-Weighted Fine-Tuning), a framework that operationalizes the theoretical insight that the KL-regularized RL objective is equivalent to importance-weighted supervised learning. DRIFT decouples rollout from optimization by sampling offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy, deriving return-based importance weights, and optimizing the policy via weighted SFT on the resulting dataset. Empirically, we demonstrate that DRIFT matches or exceeds the performance of multi-turn reinforcement learning baselines while maintaining the training efficiency and simplicity of standard supervised fine-tuning. Code is available at https://github.com/2020-qqtcg/DRIFT.

AI.tldr: DRIFT is a framework that enables efficient multi-turn LLM optimization by using importance-weighted supervised learning on offline interaction trajectories instead of expensive online RL.
AI.motivation: Online RL for multi-turn interactive LLMs is effective but prohibitively expensive, while offline supervised fine-tuning is efficient but suffers from distribution shift and behavioral collapse.
AI.method: DRIFT decouples rollout from optimization by sampling offline interaction trajectories from a fixed reference policy, deriving return-based importance weights, and optimizing the policy via weighted SFT on the resulting dataset.
AI.result: DRIFT matches or exceeds the performance of multi-turn reinforcement learning baselines while maintaining the training efficiency and simplicity of standard supervised fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: DRIFT provides an effective solution for optimizing multi-turn interactive LLM behavior that combines the benefits of online RL with the efficiency of supervised fine-tuning.

title: Balanced LoRA: Removing Parameter Invariance to Accelerate Convergence
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the most widely adopted method for fine-tuning large language models. Notably, LoRA is inherently overparameterized: multiple pairs of low-rank factors can yield the same adapted weight matrix. We show--both theoretically and empirically--that these pairs exhibit significantly different condition numbers. As a result, converging to different loss minimizers directly impacts the convergence rate of LoRA. Building on this observation, we introduce Balanced Low-Rank Adaptation (BaLoRA), a variant of LoRA that projects iterates onto a balanced manifold. This manifold improves the conditioning of the loss landscape while preserving the adapted matrix. The projection step is computationally lightweight and integrates seamlessly into existing fine-tuning pipelines. Empirically, BaLoRA converges faster than standard LoRA and achieves superior performance across a range of fine-tuning tasks.

AI.tldr: BaLoRA improves LoRA fine-tuning by projecting iterates onto a balanced manifold, leading to better conditioning of the loss landscape, faster convergence, and better performance with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA for fine-tuning large language models is inherently overparameterized (multiple low-rank factor pairs can yield same adapted weight matrix). The paper shows these pairs have significantly different condition numbers, which affects convergence rates. Addressing this imbalance could improve fine-tuning efficiency.
AI.method: Introduces Balanced Low-Rank Adaptation (BaLoRA), a variant of LoRA that projects iterates onto a balanced manifold. This projection step is lightweight and can be integrated into existing fine-tuning pipelines without disruption.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows BaLoRA converges faster than standard LoRA and achieves superior performance across a range of fine-tuning tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces BaLoRA, a method that addresses the overparameterization in LoRA fine-tuning. By projecting iterates onto a balanced manifold, BaLoRA improves conditioning of the loss landscape, leading to faster convergence and better performance than standard LoRA, with minimal computational overhead.

title: Graphical einops: bridging tensor networks and computation graphs
summary: Architecture diagrams are ubiquitous in deep learning, but they are usually only representational: the tensor-program identities they suggest are still proved by prose and tensor-axis manipulation. We introduce a formal graphical calculus for the structural fragment of tensor programming underlying einops, making such diagrams proof-enabling. Our calculus represents tensor axes as nested graded tubes around a base type. The tube boundary recovers the undirected tensor-network view of axes, while the directed interior retains the operational reading of computation graphs. The key rewrite is grade-naturality: sliding spectacles over tubes. Standard equivariance proofs become short diagrammatic derivations. We additionally demonstrate how our rewrite system may be applied to convert attention masks into pre-processing operations, recovering efficient implementations of sparse attention blocks.

AI.tldr: A formal graphical calculus for tensor programming that transforms architecture diagrams from representations into proof-enabling tools, enabling short diagrammatic derivations and practical applications like efficient sparse attention implementations.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning architecture diagrams are only representational - the tensor-program identities they suggest still require verbose prose and manual tensor-axis manipulation to prove. There is a need for a formal system that makes such diagrams truly proof-enabling.
AI.method: The authors propose representing tensor axes as nested graded tubes around a base type. The tube boundary captures the undirected tensor-network view of axes, while the directed interior maintains the operational interpretation of computation graphs. The key rewrite rule is grade-naturality, which allows sliding spectacles over tubes. This enables short diagrammatic derivations.
AI.result: The calculus enables standard equivariance proofs to become short diagrammatic derivations. Additionally, the rewrite system can be applied to convert attention masks into pre-processing operations, recovering efficient implementations of sparse attention blocks.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces a formal graphical calculus for tensor programming that transforms architecture diagrams from mere representations into proof-enabling tools. This approach elegantly bridges the gap between intuitive visualizations and rigorous mathematical reasoning.

title: Assign and Add: A Mechanistic Study of Compositional Arithmetic
summary: Large language models are able to compose skills in order to perform complex tasks, many of which might not have been seen during training. The details of how exactly this composition occurs remain elusive. In this paper, we study a mechanism for compositional generalization in transformers by considering a simple controlled setting involving variable assignment and modular addition. By partitioning our training data into disjoint sets, we observe that small transformers are able to generalize to previously unseen combinations of variables and numbers. Our mechanistic analysis shows that the same ``modular addition'' MLP module is used whether the inputs are given directly or indirectly through a separate variable assignment mechanism. We also analyze the training dynamics from an empirical lens, which reveals three phases of learning: first, modular addition is learned, then the structure required for variable assignment, and finally a refinement phase where the model generalizes to some hard sequences not seen in training. Finally, we provide a theoretical framework to explain how compositionality emerges from training dynamics. These results suggest that compositional generalization can be a natural consequence of the compositionality of internal mechanisms in~transformers.

AI.tldr: Transformers generalize compositionally by reusing internal modules (like modular addition) and learning in phases: first the module, then assignment structure, then refinement.
AI.motivation: To understand how transformers achieve compositional generalization—combining skills for novel tasks not seen during training.
AI.method: Mechanistic analysis of transformers trained on variable assignment and modular addition tasks, examining training dynamics across phases.
AI.result: Transformers reuse the same modular addition MLP module for direct and indirect (variable-assigned) inputs, and learning occurs in three phases: modular addition, variable assignment structure, then refinement.
AI.conclusion: Compositional generalization in transformers emerges naturally from the compositionality of their internal mechanisms, where learned modular components can be flexibly combined to handle novel combinations.

title: Scalable Inference-Time Annealing with Surrogate Likelihood Estimators
summary: A long standing challenge in computational chemistry and biophysics is efficiently sampling the Boltzmann distribution of molecules. Advances in generative modeling have been proposed to address the limitations of conventional sampling techniques by eliminating the computational cost of simulation. A promising direction is iteratively finetuning diffusion models along a temperature ladder whereby training data is generated via importance sampling during inference-time annealing. Unfortunately, these methods require computing a divergence over the score field to estimate importance weights, rendering them intractable for larger systems. Here we present scalable inference-time annealing (SITA), which retrains flow-based models to generate samples at progressively lower temperatures using an energy-based model to facilitate fast surrogate likelihoods. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on both Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide while avoiding costly divergence terms. Our code is available at: https://github.com/countrsignal/sita.git

AI.tldr: SITA introduces a scalable inference-time annealing method for sampling molecular Boltzmann distributions using flow-based models and energy-based models, achieving state-of-the-art performance without expensive divergence calculations.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of efficiently sampling Boltzmann distributions of molecules and overcome limitations of existing generative methods that require costly divergence calculations during inference-time annealing
AI.method: Proposes scalable inference-time annealing (SITA) which retrains flow-based models to generate samples at progressively lower temperatures using an energy-based model for fast surrogate likelihoods
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on both Alanine Dipeptide and Alanine Tripeptide while avoiding costly divergence terms
AI.conclusion: SITA presents an efficient alternative to conventional importance sampling methods in computational chemistry, achieving state-of-the-art performance while avoiding computationally expensive divergence calculations.

title: When Are Multimodal Predictions Biologically Supported? A Diagnostic Evaluation Framework
summary: Multimodal models in oncology can produce accurate predictions, but accurate prediction does not reveal whether the model has learned biology that is shared across modalities, biology confined to one modality, or spurious correlations that reflect confounders rather than genuine biology. We introduce DECAT, a model-agnostic post-hoc evaluation framework that classifies multimodal representations into four diagnostic scenarios for a given task and modality, using five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure. The framework operates on learned representations, requires no knowledge of which specific confounder is present, and returns indeterminate when the evidence is insufficient. We validate DECAT on synthetic data across four multimodal model classes (over 2,500 trained representations) and on real data from 8,979 TCGA patients, evaluating both multimodal embeddings and five pretrained pathology foundation models. Entangled models (e.g., CLIP) achieve near-perfect shared biology detection but falsely claim shared biology in the majority of cases where it is absent on real foundation model embeddings. This false claim rate increases with confound strength so that larger cohorts and stronger representations produce more confident but still incorrect diagnoses. Applied to both multimodal TCGA embeddings and five pathology foundation models without paired RNA, DECAT detects confounding invisible to AUROC without requiring the confounder labels, as confirmed by post-hoc stratification.

AI.tldr: DECAT is a framework to diagnose whether multimodal oncology models learn genuine biology or spurious correlations, revealing entangled models often incorrectly claim shared biology.
AI.motivation: Multimodal models in oncology can produce accurate predictions, but current evaluation methods cannot determine whether models have learned genuine shared biology, modality-specific biology, or spurious correlations reflecting confounders rather than true biological signals.
AI.method: Developed DECAT, a model-agnostic post-hoc evaluation framework that classifies multimodal representations into four diagnostic scenarios using five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure. Operates on learned representations without requiring knowledge of specific confounders. Validated on synthetic data across four multimodal model classes (2,500+ representations) and real data from 8,979 TCGA patients with pathology foundation models.
AI.result: Entangled models (like CLIP) achieve near-perfect shared biology detection but falsely claim shared biology in most cases where it's absent on real foundation model embeddings. False claim rate increases with confounder strength, meaning larger cohorts and stronger representations produce more confident but still incorrect diagnoses. DECAT detects confounding invisible to AUROC without requiring confounder labels, confirmed by post-hoc stratification.
AI.conclusion: DECAT provides a valuable framework for diagnosing multimodal representations in oncology, revealing that entangled models often incorrectly claim shared biology despite accurate predictions, with false claims increasing with confounder strength. The framework enables detection of confounding invisible to traditional metrics like AUROC without requiring confounder labels.

title: On the Relationship Between Activation Outliers and Feature Death in Sparse Autoencoders
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) decompose neural network activations into interpretable features, but many learned features never activate, a problem called feature death that wastes dictionary capacity and can reintroduce superposition. Death rates vary dramatically between models: near-zero on GPT-2, over 70% on AlphaFold3 with identical configurations. We find that dimension-level activation outliers (dimensions whose mean magnitude is large relative to per-token variation) cause this by shifting pre-activations at initialization based on each feature's alignment with the activation mean. Features anti-aligned with the mean receive permanently negative pre-activations and never fire. We formalize outlier severity as $γ= \|μ\|/\|σ\|$; it predicts initial death rates (Spearman $ρ= 0.89$ for dead-by-TopK, $0.82$ for dead-by-ReLU) across 454 model-layer combinations spanning language, vision, protein, and genomic models. Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires the SAE bias to learn the activation mean, a process that is prohibitively slow at high $γ$. Mean-centering (subtracting the activation mean) sidesteps this and eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models, confirming the mechanism and providing a principled basis for when and why this preprocessing step is necessary.

AI.tldr: Outlier dimensions in neural network activations cause feature death in sparse autoencoders; mean-centering activations eliminates this problem across diverse models.
AI.motivation: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are used to decompose neural network activations into interpretable features, but they suffer from "feature death" where many learned features never activate. This problem wastes dictionary capacity and can reintroduce superposition. The death rates vary dramatically between models (near-zero on GPT-2 vs over 70% on AlphaFold3 with identical configurations), suggesting some fundamental underlying mechanism needs to be understood.
AI.method: The authors investigate how dimension-level activation outliers (dimensions with large mean magnitude relative to per-token variation) cause feature death by shifting pre-activations at initialization. They formalize outlier severity as γ = ||μ||/||σ|| (mean magnitude divided by standard deviation). They test this across 454 model-layer combinations spanning language, vision, protein, and genomic models. They also examine whether mean-centering (subtracting activation mean) can eliminate outlier-induced death.
AI.result: Outlier severity γ strongly predicts initial death rates (Spearman ρ = 0.89 for dead-by-TopK, 0.82 for dead-by-ReLU) across all tested models. Features anti-aligned with activation mean receive permanently negative pre-activations and never fire. Dead features can revive during training, but recovery requires SAE bias to learn the activation mean, which is prohibitively slow at high γ. Mean-centering eliminates outlier-induced death across all tested models.
AI.conclusion: Dimension-level activation outliers are the fundamental cause of feature death in sparse autoencoders, and mean-centering activations provides a principled solution. This explains when and why preprocessing with mean-centering is necessary, with practical implications for deploying SAEs across diverse neural network architectures and domains.

title: Chem-PerturBridge: a harmonized compendium of small molecule perturbation transcriptomic effects
summary: Large perturbation models require training data encompassing chemical, cellular, and assay diversity. Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling, however, are fragmented across technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines. We introduce Chem-PerturBridge, a harmonized multi-dataset resource comprising over 37k compounds, 136 cellular contexts, and 1.25M transcriptomic samples across eight assay types, with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects. We use the resource to evaluate matched-condition agreement across datasets and replicate agreement within datasets. Matched same-compound conditions generally show weak agreement in fine-grained logFC rankings and magnitudes across most dataset pairs, often falling below same-context different-compound baselines. In contrast, logFC direction agreement is substantially more stable and usually exceeds these baselines. We further evaluate Chem-PerturBridge as a pretraining resource for compound representation learning. Under a compound-held-out OP3 evaluation split, embeddings pretrained on Chem-PerturBridge improve over L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and the descriptor-free OP3 baseline across metrics. An extensive molecule-holdout evaluation across 11 datasets further shows that models trained on Chem-PerturBridge outperform or match those that are not. Chem-PerturBridge therefore supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data.

AI.tldr: Chem-PerturBridge is a harmonized transcriptomic resource with 1.25M samples across 37k compounds and 136 cellular contexts, used to evaluate cross-dataset agreement and improve compound representation learning through pretraining.
AI.motivation: Current transcriptomic resources for small-molecule modeling are fragmented across different technologies, metadata conventions, controls, doses, and preprocessing pipelines, making large-scale perturbation modeling difficult.
AI.method: Created Chem-PerturBridge - a harmonized multi-dataset resource with standardized identifiers, metadata, and replicate-aware condition-level effects. Used this resource to evaluate matched-condition agreement across datasets and replicate agreement within datasets, and tested it as a pretraining resource for compound representation learning.
AI.result: Matched same-compound conditions show weak agreement in fine-grained logFC rankings/magnitudes across datasets (often below same-context different-compound baselines), but logFC direction agreement is substantially more stable. Chem-PerturBridge pretrained embeddings outperform L1000-only embeddings, Morgan fingerprints, and descriptor-free OP3 baseline across metrics in compound-held-out evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Chem-PerturBridge supports both diagnostic evaluation of cross-dataset signature agreement and model-oriented reuse of heterogeneous perturbation transcriptomic data, demonstrating its value as a comprehensive resource for small-molecule modeling.

title: Value Functions as Supermartingale Certificates
summary: Certification methods for stochastic systems provide sufficient proof rules, based on real-valued supermartingale certificates, to determine the almost-sure satisfaction of $ω$-regular properties (and therefore of linear temporal logic) over general state spaces, encompassing both countably infinite and continuous state spaces. Conversely, reinforcement learning (RL) methods for $ω$-regular tasks have received considerable attention, but they typically lack formal guarantees that the learned policy satisfies the specification, except possibly for finite state and action spaces. We bridge these two lines of research by establishing a novel theoretical connection: under an appropriate reward, the value function associated to a policy that almost surely satisfies an $ω$-regular property encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate for that specification. Our results, validated experimentally on finite Markov decision processes, hold for finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces, suggesting a principled route to certificate synthesis via RL.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a theoretical connection between reinforcement learning and formal certification methods for ω-regular properties, showing that value functions from RL can encode formal supermartingale certificates for temporal logic specifications.
AI.motivation: There's a disconnect between formal certification methods (which provide rigorous guarantees for stochastic systems) and RL methods for ω-regular tasks (which typically lack formal guarantees). Certification methods work for general state spaces but RL guarantees are limited to finite spaces. The paper aims to bridge these two research lines.
AI.method: The authors establish a theoretical connection showing that under appropriate reward shaping, the value function associated with a policy that almost surely satisfies an ω-regular property encodes a Streett supermartingale certificate for that specification.
AI.result: The theoretical results are validated experimentally on finite Markov Decision Processes, but the theoretical connection holds for finite, countably infinite, and continuous state spaces.
AI.conclusion: The work suggests a principled route to certificate synthesis via reinforcement learning, potentially enabling RL methods to provide formal guarantees for temporal logic specifications across general state spaces.

title: The Dynamic-Probabilistic Consistency Gap in Chaotic Surrogate Modeling
summary: Dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR) aims to learn surrogate models that capture the dynamics underlying time-series data. Reliably deploying these surrogates requires uncertainty estimates consistent with the learned dynamics. We expose a dynamic-probabilistic consistency (DPC) gap: the pursuit of finite-horizon probabilistic objectives can degrade dynamics or decouple predictive uncertainty from the local tangent dynamics it ought to reflect. We isolate three mechanisms behind this gap: core collapse, noise masking, and blind uncertainty. Specifically, we show that open-loop Gaussian rollout objectives can penalize Jacobian-generated covariance growth in chaotic systems, encouraging optimization shortcuts that weaken physical expansion or decouple uncertainty from it. To mitigate this gap, we propose KAFFEE (Kalman-Aware Framework For Ergodic Emulation), a differentiable extended Kalman filter-based training framework that evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals (innovations) while transporting covariance through learned local Jacobians. On stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96, KAFFEE reduces the identified failure modes, improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants relative to open-loop objectives, and maintains competitive predictive scores. We further show that the DPC gap appears when probabilistically adapting a DSR foundation model across 13 chaotic systems, where KAFFEE enables in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.

AI.tldr: KAFFEE addresses the dynamic-probabilistic consistency gap in dynamical systems reconstruction by using a differentiable extended Kalman filter framework that evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals while transporting covariance through learned Jacobians, improving uncertainty estimates and preserving learned dynamics.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies a "dynamic-probabilistic consistency (DPC) gap" where finite-horizon probabilistic objectives in dynamical systems reconstruction can degrade learned dynamics or decouple predictive uncertainty from the local tangent dynamics it should reflect. Current methods using open-loop Gaussian rollout objectives can penalize Jacobian-generated covariance growth in chaotic systems, encouraging optimization shortcuts that weaken physical expansion or decouple uncertainty from it.
AI.method: The authors propose KAFFEE (Kalman-Aware Framework For Ergodic Emulation), a differentiable extended Kalman filter-based training framework. KAFFEE evaluates likelihood on local predictive residuals (innovations) while transporting covariance through learned local Jacobians. This approach addresses the identified failure modes of core collapse, noise masking, and blind uncertainty.
AI.result: On stochastic hyperchaotic Lorenz-96, KAFFEE reduces the identified failure modes, improves reconstruction of dynamical invariants relative to open-loop objectives, and maintains competitive predictive scores. The method also addresses the DPC gap when probabilistically adapting a DSR foundation model across 13 chaotic systems, enabling in-context Bayesian filtering while largely preserving zero-shot dynamics.
AI.conclusion: KAFFEE successfully mitigates the dynamic-probabilistic consistency gap in dynamical systems reconstruction by aligning probabilistic training objectives with the underlying dynamical constraints. The framework improves uncertainty calibration and preserves learned dynamics while maintaining predictive performance, making surrogate models more reliable for deployment.

title: Functional Attention: From Pairwise Affinities to Functional Correspondences
comment: 26 pages, 12 figures. Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces, or operator learning, is essential for many machine learning applications. Although transformer-based operators are popular, they often rely on token-wise attention. These methods treat continuous fields as discrete tokens and usually ignore the global functional structure. We introduce \emph{Functional Attention}, which reinterprets attention as a functional correspondence between adaptive bases. Inspired by geometric functional maps, our method replaces softmax affinities with structured linear operators. This yields a compact, generalizable, resolution-invariant representation that explicitly captures global dependencies. Experiments demonstrate that \emph{Functional Attention} can match state-of-the-art performance in many operator learning tasks, including solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, and regression, while remaining robust to varying discretizations. Project page is available at https://github.com/xjffff/FUNCATTN.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Functional Attention, a novel attention mechanism for operator learning that reinterprets attention as functional correspondence between adaptive bases, replacing token-wise softmax with structured linear operators to capture global functional structure in a resolution-invariant way.
AI.motivation: Current transformer-based operators for learning mappings between infinite-dimensional function spaces (operator learning) rely on token-wise attention that treats continuous fields as discrete tokens and ignores global functional structure, limiting their ability to capture true functional dependencies.
AI.method: Introduces Functional Attention, inspired by geometric functional maps, which reinterprets attention as functional correspondence between adaptive bases. It replaces softmax affinities with structured linear operators to create a compact, resolution-invariant representation that explicitly captures global dependencies.
AI.result: Experiments show that Functional Attention matches state-of-the-art performance in various operator learning tasks including solving PDEs, 3D segmentation, and regression, while demonstrating robustness to varying discretizations.
AI.conclusion: Functional Attention provides an effective approach to operator learning that captures global functional structure in a resolution-invariant manner, addressing limitations of token-wise attention methods while maintaining competitive performance across diverse applications.

title: Effective Biological Representation Learning by Masking Gene Expression
comment: 31 pages, 11 figures. Preprint; presented at ICLR 2026 2nd Workshop on Foundation Models for Science: Real-World Impact and Science-First Design
summary: RNA sequencing produces rich and diverse datasets of gene expression, offering compelling insights into cellular state and function that have many applications in drug discovery. Modeling such data is challenging due to inherent technical noise and experimental batch effects, as evidenced by many existing transcriptomic foundation models (FMs) underperforming relative to linear baselines. Such results raise the question of whether deep representation learning provides a distinct advantage over the direct use of raw transcript counts. Our work explores this by developing a new self-supervised model, TxFM, with a focus on inductive representation learning evaluations. TxFM employs a masked autoencoding approach tailored to diverse RNA-seq count data, and our ablation study empirically identifies crucial architecture configurations required for strong transfer performance. Additionally, we curate a public training corpus, DiverseRNA-1.4M, and find that TxFM trained on this curated dataset yields high-fidelity gene representations that outperform FMs trained on atlas-scale corpora over 100x larger. Overall, our results indicate that inductive self-supervised learning is a viable modeling approach for transcriptomics representation, provided a careful synthesis of model architecture and training data curation.

AI.tldr: TxFM is a new self-supervised model for RNA-seq data that outperforms larger foundation models when trained on a carefully curated dataset (DiverseRNA-1.4M), showing inductive self-supervised learning works for transcriptomics with proper architecture and data curation.
AI.motivation: Existing transcriptomic foundation models underperform relative to linear baselines, raising questions about whether deep representation learning provides advantages over raw transcript counts. This work explores whether self-supervised learning can yield better transcriptomic representations.
AI.method: Developed TxFM, a new self-supervised model using masked autoencoding approach tailored to diverse RNA-seq count data. Conducted ablation study to identify crucial architecture configurations. Created DiverseRNA-1.4M public training corpus with curated RNA-seq data.
AI.result: TxFM trained on DiverseRNA-1.4M yields high-fidelity gene representations that outperform foundation models trained on datasets over 100x larger. The ablation study identified key architecture configurations needed for strong transfer performance.
AI.conclusion: Inductive self-supervised learning is a viable modeling approach for transcriptomics representation, but requires careful synthesis of model architecture and training data curation.

title: Giving Sensors a Voice: Multimodal JEPA for Semantic Time-Series Embeddings
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, accepted at ICML 2026. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2505.14543
summary: Transformer-based architectures have advanced sequence modeling in language and vision, yet general-purpose representation learning for heterogeneous multivariate time series remains underexplored. We introduce CHARM (Channel-Aware Representation Model), which incorporates channel-level textual descriptions into a Transformer encoder equivariant to channel order. CHARM is trained with a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) and a novel loss promoting informative, temporally stable embeddings; latent-space prediction encourages robustness to sensor noise while description-aware gating provides interpretability through learned inter-channel relationships. Across anomaly detection, classification, and short- and long-term forecasting, the learned embeddings achieve strong performance using only a linear probe. Performance is driven primarily by the JEPA objective and conditioning architecture, with text descriptions serving as channel identifiers for cross-dataset generalization.

AI.tldr: CHARM is a Transformer-based model that incorporates channel-level textual descriptions to learn general-purpose representations for heterogeneous multivariate time series, achieving strong performance across multiple tasks with linear probes.
AI.motivation: While Transformer architectures have advanced sequence modeling in language and vision, there's limited exploration of general-purpose representation learning for heterogeneous multivariate time series data.
AI.method: CHARM uses a Transformer encoder equivariant to channel order, incorporates channel-level textual descriptions via description-aware gating, and is trained with a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) with novel loss functions promoting informative, temporally stable embeddings.
AI.result: The learned embeddings achieve strong performance across anomaly detection, classification, and short-/long-term forecasting tasks using only linear probes. The JEPA objective and conditioning architecture are primary performance drivers.
AI.conclusion: CHARM demonstrates effective representation learning for multivariate time series by incorporating channel descriptions into Transformer architectures, enabling cross-dataset generalization through text descriptions serving as channel identifiers.

title: A Tight Theory of Error Feedback Algorithms in Distributed Optimization
summary: Communication costs are a major bottleneck in distributed learning and first-order optimization. A common approach to alleviate this issue is to compress the gradient information exchanged between agents. However, such compression typically degrades the convergence guarantees of gradient-based methods. Error feedback mechanisms provide a simple and computationally cheap remedy for this issue, but numerous variants have been proposed, and their relative performance remains poorly understood. This paper provides tight convergence analyses for two of the main error-feedback algorithms from the literature, the classic Error Feedback method (EF) and Error Feedback 21 (EF21), by identifying optimal step-size choices and constructing optimal Lyapunov functions tailored to each method. The results hold independently of the number of agents and recover the known best guarantees possible in the single-agent regime.

AI.tldr: Tight convergence analysis of two error-feedback algorithms (EF and EF21) for distributed optimization with gradient compression, identifying optimal step-sizes and Lyapunov functions.
AI.motivation: Communication costs are a major bottleneck in distributed learning, and gradient compression helps but degrades convergence guarantees. Error feedback mechanisms provide a simple remedy, but many variants exist with poorly understood relative performance.
AI.method: The paper constructs optimal Lyapunov functions tailored to each error-feedback algorithm (EF and EF21). It identifies optimal step-size choices through these Lyapunov analyses.
AI.result: The analysis provides tight convergence guarantees for EF and EF21 algorithms. The results identify optimal step-size choices and show the guarantees hold for any number of agents while matching the best known single-agent performance.
AI.conclusion: This paper provides tight convergence analyses for EF and EF21, identifying optimal step-size choices and constructing optimal Lyapunov functions. The results hold for any number of agents and recover the best known single-agent guarantees.
